narrative_blocks.header: He dazzled French Vogue with citrus and ink
narrative_blocks.body: For a few brilliant years around 1950 his drawings lit up French Vogue, all citrus yellow and shocking pink under a draughtsman's easy line. He designed for ballet, theater, and films, including a Judy Garland musical.

narrative_blocks.header: He drew the covers of his wife's novels
narrative_blocks.body: Even after their tempestuous marriage ended he illustrated the jackets of Theodora's books, and she called him her estranged but still beloved Tom. His career, crowded with talent, never quite cohered before his early death.

bio.bio_blurb: Vogue illustrator; Theodora Keogh's first husband

narrative_blocks.header: He dazzled French Vogue with citrus and ink

bio.bio_blurb: Vogue illustrator; Theodora Keogh's first husband

narrative_blocks.body: Between 1950 and 1962 she published nine novels of psychological menace, full of characters with hidden and dangerous second selves, then stopped writing for good. Patricia Highsmith, who almost never reviewed anyone, praised her first book.

narrative_blocks.header: An early voice of lesbian pulp fiction
narrative_blocks.body: Her frank treatment of gay and lesbian lives made her one of the daring postwar writers who opened American fiction to those themes. Long forgotten, her books were rediscovered and reissued in the early two-thousands.

narrative_blocks.header: A debutante who danced in South America
narrative_blocks.body: She came out in New York society in 1937, then made her living as a professional dancer in South America and Canada. She gave it up in 1945 to marry an artist and move to Paris.

narrative_blocks.header: She played down the Roosevelt name
narrative_blocks.body: She wrote under her first husband's surname and downplayed her famous lineage, wanting the work judged on its own merits. She married three times and spent her last years alone in the North Carolina woods with cats and chickens.
